blueprints.title: Refactor existing code safely without changing intended behavior
blueprints.goal: Restructure or simplify existing code while preserving intended behavior, keeping scope tight, and verifying that the refactor does not introduce side effects or speculative abstractions.

preconditions.description: There is existing code with known behavior that should be preserved while improving structure, readability, or maintainability.
The current behavior can be inspected through tests, call sites, invariants, or direct code reading before changes are made.

steps.title: Define the behavior boundary
steps.instruction: State what behavior must remain unchanged: outputs, side effects, invariants, public interfaces, ordering constraints, or error behavior that callers rely on. Identify what is in scope to improve and what must stay out of scope.

steps.title: Inspect the current structure and pain points
steps.instruction: Read the existing implementation and identify the concrete pain points motivating the refactor: duplication, mixed responsibilities, unclear naming, tangled control flow, or oversized units. Keep the diagnosis tied to the current code, not hypothetical future needs.

steps.title: Find evidence of existing behavior
steps.instruction: Identify the strongest available evidence for current behavior: tests, stable call sites, invariants, fixtures, expected outputs, or explicit constraints in code. If coverage is weak, note which part of the behavior boundary is least protected.
expectedEvidence: weakly protected areas noted if any

steps.title: Choose the smallest safe refactor cut
steps.instruction: Pick the smallest structural change that addresses the real pain point without expanding scope. Prefer one cohesive extraction or simplification over a sweeping rewrite, and avoid adding abstractions that are only justified by hypothetical reuse.

steps.title: Apply the refactor
steps.instruction: Perform the behavior-preserving restructuring itself: simplify flow, extract cohesive pieces, rename for clarity, or remove local duplication while keeping the defined boundary intact.

steps.title: Verify behavior is preserved
steps.instruction: Verify the refactor against the strongest behavior evidence available. Confirm that outputs, interfaces, side effects, and key invariants still hold. If anything changed intentionally, call it out explicitly instead of presenting it as a pure refactor.
expectedEvidence: changed behavior explicitly called out if any

steps.title: Report refactor result
steps.instruction: Summarize what structural problem was improved, what behavior boundary was preserved, what evidence supports safety, and any residual risk or follow-up work that was deliberately left out of scope.

branches.condition: If behavior evidence is weak, narrow the refactor cut further and make the uncertainty explicit rather than expanding the change anyway.
If the change alters behavior, stop calling it a pure refactor and either reduce scope or explicitly treat the work as a behavior change with separate justification.

expectedEvidence.description: The intended behavior boundary and refactor scope were defined before changing structure.
Concrete current pain points and the strongest behavior evidence were identified.
The refactor was kept to the smallest safe structural cut and verified against existing behavior evidence.
The final answer explains what was improved, what stayed invariant, and what risk remains.

failureModes.title: Scope creep disguised as refactor
signals: adjacent modules are changed without need
new abstractions are introduced for hypothetical future reuse
multiple independent cleanups are bundled together
failureModes.mitigation: Keep the change focused on one structural pain point and choose the smallest safe refactor cut.

failureModes.title: Behavior changed without being acknowledged
signals: outputs or side effects shift but the change is still described as a pure refactor
public interface or ordering changes are not called out
failureModes.mitigation: Define the behavior boundary first and explicitly reclassify the work if behavior changes are introduced.

failureModes.title: Refactoring without evidence of current behavior
signals: no tests, invariants, or stable call-site expectations are consulted
safety is asserted from intuition alone
failureModes.mitigation: Use the strongest available behavior evidence before and after the change, and narrow scope if evidence is weak.

failureModes.title: Cleaning up based on hypothetical design goals
signals: future flexibility drives the refactor more than current pain points
generalized abstractions appear before a real need is shown
failureModes.mitigation: Tie each structural change to a concrete current pain point and avoid speculative abstraction.
